title: 10 best herbal teas for sour taste in mouth

remedy_properties: Antimicrobial properties: Peppermint tea's antimicrobial properties help to reduce the growth of bacteria in the mouth, which can cause bad breath and a sour taste, by killing off harmful microorganisms that contribute to these issues.

Digestive soothing properties: The menthol content in peppermint tea helps to soothe digestive issues such as indigestion, bloating, and gas, which are common causes of a sour taste in the mouth, by relaxing the muscles in the stomach and reducing inflammation.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Peppermint tea's anti-inflammatory properties help to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can cause a sour or metallic taste, by inhibiting the production of pro-inflammatory compounds that contribute to these issues.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita, also known as peppermint, teas helps with sour taste in mouth because it contains menthol, a natural compound that has antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. When consumed, menthol helps to reduce bacteria and inflammation in the mouth, which can cause a sour taste. Additionally, menthol's cooling sensation can help to neutralize the acidity and ease discomfort associated with a sour taste, providing quick and effective relief. This makes peppermint tea a popular remedy for a sour mouth.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Menthol helps to neutralize the sour taste in the mouth by numbing the taste buds, temporarily reducing the sensation of sourness.
Menthone: Menthone has antibacterial properties that help to reduce the presence of bacteria in the mouth, which can contribute to sour taste, thereby alleviating the issue.

Limonene: Limonene has anti-inflammatory properties that may help to reduce inflammation in the mouth, which can cause a sour taste, and also has a cleansing effect that can help to eliminate bacteria and other irritants.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Leaves: The most commonly used part of Mentha x piperita for teas is due to their high concentration of menthol and menthone, which help to eliminate the sour taste in the mouth.
Buds: Buds: Buds are used to make teas because they contain a higher concentration of menthol and menthone compared to the leaves, making them more potent in neutralizing sour taste.
Stems: Stems: Stems are used to make teas as they also contain menthol and menthone, although in smaller quantities compared to leaves and buds, but are still effective in reducing sour taste.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh mentha x piperita leaves in the early morning to capture the highest essential oil content.
Rinse the harvested leaves with cold water to remove any dirt or debris.
Steep 1 teaspoon of fresh mentha x piperita leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 minutes to release the flavors.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup to remove the leaves from the liquid.
Let the tea cool to room temperature before serving to enhance the flavor and aroma.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Wild marjoram tea's antioxidants help reduce inflammation and neutralize free radicals, which can contribute to a sour taste in the mouth by reducing the risk of gum disease and other oral infections.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in wild marjoram tea, such as thymol, can help reduce swelling and irritation in the mouth, tongue, and throat, which may be contributing to a sour taste.

Antimicrobial properties: Wild marjoram tea's antimicrobial properties can help kill bacteria and other microorganisms that can cause bad breath, gum disease, and other oral health issues, all of which can contribute to a sour taste in the mouth.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Origanum vulgare, also known as wild marjoram, teas helps with sour taste in mouth because it contains antimicrobial properties that combat the bacteria causing halitosis and oral infections. The tea's antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties help reduce inflammation and soothe the mucous membranes in the mouth, neutralizing the acidity and sour taste. Additionally, its antifungal properties prevent the growth of fungi that contribute to bad breath and a sour taste. remedy_constituents: Carvacrol: Carvacrol, a monoterpenic phenol, acts as an antimicrobial agent, which can help reduce bacteria that cause bad breath and sour taste in the mouth.

Thymol: Thymol, a phenolic compound, has antimicrobial properties that can help eliminate bacteria and other microorganisms in the mouth that cause bad breath and sour taste.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Origanum vulgare are commonly used to make teas that help reduce acidity and soothe a sour taste in the mouth due to their antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties.
Roots: The roots of Origanum vulgare are used in teas to help alleviate a sour taste in the mouth by reducing inflammation and promoting oral health due to their antimicrobial properties.
Stems: The stems of Origanum vulgare are also used in teas to help soothe a sour taste in the mouth by reducing inflammation and promoting oral well-being due to their antiseptic properties.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh origanum vulgare leaves in the morning after the dew has evaporated and before the heat of the day sets in.
Dry the harvested leaves in a low-temperature oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 2 hours or until crispy.
Chop 1 tablespoon of dried origanum vulgare leaves into small pieces using a pair of sharp kitchen scissors.
Steep 1 teaspoon of chopped origanum vulgare leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5 to 7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup and discard the solids.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 10-20 fresh or dried roots of glycyrrhiza glabra for tea making to ensure quality and potency.
Dry the roots in a warm oven at 150-200°f for 1-2 hours to remove moisture content completely.
Grind 1-2 teaspoons of dried roots into fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Steep 1 teaspoon of the powder in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release active compounds.
Strain and serve the tea immediately after brewing to preserve flavor and nutritional benefits.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2-3 teaspoons of dried zingiber officinale root and place it in a tea infuser or a heat-resistant cup.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to sour taste in mouth that are treated with herbal teas are gum inflammation, bad breath, and digestive issues. Ginger tea has anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe inflamed gums and reduce bacterial growth, while peppermint tea's antibacterial properties combat bad breath. Tea blends containing fennel, coriander, and cumin may also alleviate digestive discomforts like bloating and indigestion, which can cause an unpleasant sensation in the mouth.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for sour taste in mouth are a natural remedy that helps alleviate the discomfort and pain associated with a sour taste, also known as metalic taste. This condition, often caused by dry mouth, acid reflux, or certain medications, can significantly impact daily life. The best herbal teas for sour taste in mouth include Zingiber officinale, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Mentha x piperita, Echinacea purpurea, Cinnamomum verum, Cinchona officinalis, Origanum vulgare, Thymus vulgaris, Cymbopogon citratus, and Rosmarinus officinalis. If left untreated, a sour taste in mouth can lead to social withdrawal, decreased appetite, and poor oral health, ultimately affecting one's overall quality of life.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with sour taste in mouth is peppermint, chamomile, and licorice root tea. Peppermint tea helps to freshen breath and reduce inflammation, while chamomile tea soothes the mouth and calms the digestive system. Licorice root tea, rich in glycyrrhizin, has anti-inflammatory properties that help to balance the pH levels in the mouth, reducing the sour taste. Drinking these teas together can provide a soothing and healing effect, promoting a healthy and pleasant mouth environment.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Avicenna journal of phytomedicine, the consumption of sour tea has been found to have a positive impact on lipid profiles in individuals with polygenic dyslipidemia. The study involved 43 adults with polygenic dyslipidemia who were randomly assigned to an intervention group receiving two cups of sour tea daily and a control group trained in lifestyle modifications. After 12 weeks, the intervention group showed a significant decrease in total cholesterol, HDL-C, and LDL-C by up to 9.46%, 8.33%, and 9.80%, respectively, indicating that sour tea may help regulate lipid profiles and alleviate symptoms of sour taste in mouth associated with dyslipidemia.
